items.q: Why is injection moulding suited to high-volume production?
items.correct: Tooling cost is high but per-unit cost is very low at scale.
items.misconception: It is the cheapest method for any quantity.
items.random_wrong: It is the only way to make plastic items.
items.why: Students think a 'good' process is good for everything. Injection moulding has expensive moulds - it's only economical when costs are spread over thousands or millions of parts.

items.q: What is FEA used for in product design?
items.correct: Simulating how a product will respond to forces, heat, or vibration before manufacture.
items.misconception: Final exam analysis of student designs.
items.random_wrong: Counting the elements in a chemical compound.
items.why: Students unfamiliar with the acronym guess at it. FEA = Finite Element Analysis: dividing a part into many small elements and simulating physical behaviour computationally - saves prototyping costs.

items.q: Which of the 6 Rs has the GREATEST environmental benefit?
items.correct: Refuse or Reduce (preventing the impact at source).
items.why: Students treat recycling as the gold standard, but recycling uses energy and downgrades materials. The hierarchy is Refuse, Reduce, Reuse, Repair, Recycle, Rethink - PREVENTION beats reprocessing.

items.q: Whose 2005 Double Diamond models the design process?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: UK Design Council. “IDEO” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which technique systematically alters product attributes?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: SCAMPER. “TRIZ” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many diamonds are in the Double Diamond model?
items.why: Exact answer: 2 diamonds. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
